observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to: a. Direct the City Accountant to maintain a special account for the 20 percent DF by maintaining complete set of subsidiary ledgers under the GF Proper for easy monitoring of the receipt, transfer and utilization of the said fund and to enable the generation of the financial statements which can aid in decision-making and other purposes; and b. Direct the City Treasurer, upon authorization from the Sangguniang Panlungsod, to open a separate depository account for the 20 percent DF or the fund may be deposited together with the funds of the GF proper provided a Subsidiary Ledger is maintained for each account to record the receipts, transfers and expenditures involving said fund, pursuant to Section 313 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented A separate bank account has not yet been opened.
observations.reason: A separate bank account has not yet been opened.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to the following: a. In case the MOA is not available, coordinate with the Source Agency to determine the course of action for the remaining unexpended/unutilized balances; and b. In the event that the funding source agency could not be determined, recommend to the City Mayor to file a request for write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented No line of communication with source agencies was established yet, so no action was made as to the remaining balances/dormant accounts. Proper assessment of these accounts will be processed as soon as proper communication has been established with the appropriate agencies.
observations.reason: No line of communication with source agencies was established yet, so no action was made as to the remaining balances/dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the inventory taking and complete all planned activities/procedures in the approved PIP and prepare the RPCPPE pursuant to Item 6.2.10 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; b) Require the General Services Office (GSO) to update its property records based on the result of the physical inventory and reconcile with the accounting records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts pursuant to Item 5.12 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and c) Instruct the GSO to initiate disposal procedures for the unlocated or non-existing PPEs following the procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Actual physical count was conducted and reports prepared. However, procedures for the one-time cleansing of the PPE were not made. GSO was ordered to maintain an updated records of all properties and to constantly reconcile with the accounting office. No disposal procedures were made.
observations.reason: Procedures for the one-time cleansing of the PPE were not undertaken; reconciliation and disposal still pending.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor and the City Accountant: a) Enforce immediate settlement of outstanding cash advances totaling ₱13,837,662.98 pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 97-02 and 2012-004 dated February 10, 1997 and November 28, 2012, respectively; b) Ensure that no additional cash advances shall be granted to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance granted to him/her has been settled or a proper accounting thereof has been made pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445; and c) Strictly impose the withholding of salary and the institution of other sanctions as provided under paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Some of the outstanding cash advances were from employees who have retired or separated from service and even deceased persons that collection proves impossible. Grant of additional cash advances for those with outstanding accounts, unless liquidation is submitted, is strictly enforced.
observations.reason: Some outstanding advances are from retired/separated/deceased employees making collection difficult or impossible.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and/or the City Treasurer: a) Prioritize the reconciliation of intra-agency accounts (Due from/to Other Funds) to eliminate the discrepancy by tracing and identifying the errors/omissions/mis-postings in the recording of prior years’ transactions; b) Effect the necessary correcting/adjusting entries to arrive at reliable balances of the two reciprocal accounts; and c) Conduct regular reconciliation of intra-agency accounts to avoid accumulation of intra-agency reciprocal account discrepancies.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented Reconciliation of the said accounts is ongoing. Reconciliation is ongoing, thus correcting/adjusting entries could not be made.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing and correcting/adjusting entries could not yet be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Submit specific legal basis for the grant of benefits, awards and rewards to all City officials and employees. b. Submit information or records that monetary awards were granted only when the suggestion, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts result in monetary saving and the monetary awards granted did not exceed 20% of the savings generated pursuant to Item 6 of CSC MC No. 01; c. Submit Agency’s Annual Work and Financial Plan and budget showing at least 5% of the HRD Funds allocated for the PRAISE is incorporated.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Difficulty in following up the submission of documents due to change in leadership. No updates were communicated to the office as to its submission.
observations.reason: Difficulty in following up submission of documents due to change in leadership; no updates communicated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor prioritize inclusion of the project under the General Fund budget in the ensuing year for the development and improvement of the lots acquired for the City Employees Housing Projects, so that the intended beneficiaries could have a house of their own in order to boost their morale and uplift the standard of living of the City employees.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The City is already making progress in the procedures necessary for the eventual distribution of the said lots. Moreover, an ordinance is required for its final distribution. Currently, a portion of which is to be utilized as location for temporary market stalls while the public market is under major renovation.
observations.reason: Ordinance required for final distribution; partial procedures ongoing; lots under temporary use.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor submit specific legal basis for the grant of Other Personal Benefits, Awards and Rewards to the City employees for CY 2017. Henceforth, he must ensure that benefits or incentives granted to City officials and employees are expressly provided by law, and other statutory authority and the rules and regulations promulgated by competent authority pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 13, 2013.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The previous administration has taken responsibility in the submission of all the necessary legal documents related to such.
observations.reason: Previous administration responsible for submission of necessary legal documents; follow-up pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Local Government should enforce immediate settlement of disallowances and charges.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Balances were from prior years and collectability has become a great difficulty due to lack of records or turnover thereof. Implemented Strict adherence to regulations and policies is now being implemented to avoid occurrence of audit disallowances.
observations.reason: Balances from prior years; collectability difficult due to lack of records/turnover.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant to prepare necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of affected accounts for fair presentation in the financial statements, as follows:
Due from NGAs ₱5,088,200.15
CIP-Building and Other Structures ₱5,088,200.15

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to correct the overstated account balances as of December 31, 2024 on the transfer of unexpended Mitigation Fund in the Capital Outlay of DRRMF for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
a. Prepare an adjusting entry to correct the error amounting to ₱2.098 million made in the recognition of payment made by the SEF to the General Fund for the remittance to BIR by the GF and conduct a detailed reconciliation for the remaining accounts with abnormal/negative balances totaling ₱175,792.61 to determine the underlying causes and to ascertain its correctness and reliability for fair presentation in the financial statements; and
b. Conduct a review of historical data, prioritizing the accounts with balances carried over from prior years (e.g., CY 2016), to identify the original causes of the negative balances. Otherwise, if documentation is unavailable, employ alternative methods to further evaluate the transactions.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the Acting City Treasurer to timely request from the depository bank, copies of the DM/CM and furnish the City Accountant for the immediate recording of all reconciling items in the year of incurrence and to reflect reliable Cash-in-Bank balance in the financial statements.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:
a) Designate personnel in-charge in the verification on the existence of the non-moving Inventory account balances totaling ₱6.682 million and for the preparation of the corresponding report for necessary adjustments by the City Accountant on the Inventory and other affected accounts.
b) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct semestral physical count on all supplies and materials for the preparation and submission of the RCPI as other basis in the adjustments on the Inventory and other affected accounts to ensure the existence, accuracy and reliability of the balances for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant and the Acting City Treasurer to trace the differences of the RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred Income Tax accounts and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to reconcile the loan balances per accounting records with the LBP to determine the correct balance, and prepare any necessary adjustments to accurately reflect the Loans Payable account in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to review the composition of the Construction in Progress account and make the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts, including the computation of depreciation expense to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts pursuant to the Section 50 on the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation and the City Mayor agreed to direct the Accountant to determine the estimated useful lives and compute the depreciation expense of Infrastructure Assets totaling ₱180.477 million and accordingly adjust the useful lives of these assets and charge appropriately their value to the remaining useful lives to come up with the correct and fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Difficulty in the identification and reconciliation of accounts due to lack of records as a basis for such. Coordination with the Engineers' Office was made to identify these projects. Further, one of the possible difficulties encountered is that most of these projects might also be overlaps of various repeat works. Infrastructure assets recently recognized are provided with depreciation.
observations.reason: Difficulty in the identification and reconciliation of accounts due to lack of records as a basis for such. Coordination with the Engineers' Office was made to identify these projects. Further, one of the possible difficulties encountered is that most of these projects might also be overlaps of various repeat works. Infrastructure assets recently recognized are provided with depreciation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Direct the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of the physical count on PPE, prepare and submit the RPCPPE and finish all other procedures in the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances; and b. Direct the GSO in coordination with the City Accountant, to gradually reconcile the discrepancy between the property and accounting records on PPE based on the result of the physical inventory and ensure that the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Inventory Committee has completed the inventory count and submitted the inventory report. However, the procedures in the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts were not yet initiated. Reconciliation is being made, yet difficulty was encountered due to a lack of records and proper documentation.
observations.reason: The procedures in the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts were not yet initiated. Reconciliation is being made, yet difficulty was encountered due to a lack of records and proper documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The City Mayor and the concerned officials observe the fundamental principles governing the financial transactions and operations of any government agency (under Section 4(1) of PD No. 1445 which states no money shall be paid out of any public treasury except in pursuance of an appropriation law or other specific statutory authority); b. The City Treasurer, together with the City Budget Officer and City Accountant thoroughly review the amount to be presented in the Statement of Receipts and Expenditures in order to reflect the proper balances that could aid in the monitoring of available cash balance; c. The City Treasurer adhere with Section 337 of RA No. 7160 in the disbursement of funds and ensure that payments are controlled as to availability of applicable fund and not as to available cash balance; and d. The City officials who certified and approved the disbursements with insufficient cash shall be liable for the incurrence of cash overdraft/deficit.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Strict adherence to regulations and policies is now being implemented to avoid occurrence of audit disallowances. The Recommendati ons were restated in this report.
observations.reason: Strict adherence to regulations and policies is now being implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to: a. Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that the NOA is properly received by the winning bidder by indicating the date of receipt since such date will be the start of the period within which the winning bidder should post the performance security to guarantee faithful performance of its contract period; and b. Direct the BAC to observe the procurement rules and timelines required under RA No. 9184 and require the winning bidders to post the required performance security and enter into contract with the PE or the City within 10 calendar days from receipt by the winning bidders of the Notices of Award pursuant to Sections 37.2.1 and 39.1 of the IRR of RA No. 9184 since the said stages of procurement process are mandatory in nature except only for justifiable cause pursuant to Section 65.1 (b) of the IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Accountant and/or authorized representative together with the City Treasurer agreed to ascertain and ensure that the cash advance for a given project or activity chargeable to CF granted to SDO is covered by sufficient amount of bond, in accordance with Item 4.1.1.3 of Treasury Circular No. 1-2022 dated May 30, 2022 and Item 6.1.3 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Accountant or the authorized representative agreed to submit for post audit the cash advance vouchers for Confidential Fund, with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents, to the concerned COA Audit Team Leader within seven days after release of check, pursuant to Item 6.1.8.4 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to remit the five percent DPWH share from the income derived from building permit fees and other charges from September 2022 to December 31, 2023 and the years thereafter in compliance with Item 2.3 of DPWH-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 001 dated July 4, 2013 and immediately notify the DPWH Secretary through the NBCDO of its remittance so that the required validation could be undertaken by said office.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Government adhere to the guidelines on the sources of payment of honoraria to the BAC Chairman and members, BAC Secretariat and BAC TWG which shall be sourced from the collections from successfully completed procurement projects and funded by savings from the local budget after satisfying mandatory requirement provided under DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-5A, as amended by DBM Circular No. 2007-3 dated November 29, 2007.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

Confirm with the Province of Negros Occidental and La Carlota City regarding non-moving balances of fund transfer recorded in the books. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Ensure that projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund are specific and itemized pursuant to Article 454(d) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 7160 and the projects are well planned, procurement and implementation ready pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020; and b) Secure the approval or authorization from the Sangguniang Panlungsod prior to the implementation of PPAs funded out of the lump-sum appropriation under the 20% Development Fund Plan pursuant to COA Memorandum No. 2010-014 dated April 22, 2010.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Items in the 20% Development Fund are itemized and specified. Approval from the Sangguniang Panlungsod is secured prior to implementation.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The Inventory Committee conduct a complete physical inventory of all City’s properties and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I for the Audit Team to have ample time to validate the reported balances of PPE; and
The City Accountant and the GSO implement the immediate reconciliation of their records inasmuch as this deficiency has long been observed and no concrete action was undertaken to resolve the discrepancy and determine the status of the unidentified PPE items totaling ₱71,000,329.09 per GSO inventory report.
Unserviceable properties valued at ₱58,231,322.14 were still carried in the books as well as in the inventory report due to the failure of management to undertake disposal procedures cited in Section 79 of P.D. 1445, thus, resulting in the overstatement of PPE and Government Equity accounts by the same amount.
Section 79 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 provides:
“When government property has become unserviceable for any cause, or is no longer needed, it shall upon application of the officer accountable therefore, be inspected by the head of the agency or his duly authorized representative in the presence of the auditor concerned 	and, if found to be valueless or unsalable, it may be destroyed in their 	presence.  If found to be valuable, it may be sold at public 	auction to the 	highest bidder under the supervision of the proper committee on 	award 	or similar body in the presence of the auditor concerned or other duly authorized representative of the Commission on Audit.”
Further, Title V, Rule 21, Section 162 of COA Circular No. 92-386 states:
“When supplies or property of any local government unit have become unserviceable for any cause or are no longer needed, the same shall be disposed of in accordance with the procedures prescribed in these rules and regulations.”
Scrutiny of the Inventory Report submitted by the GSO revealed several unserviceable PPE items totaling₱58,231,322.14, summarized as follows:
The Inventory Report also showed that some unserviceable properties are still located in the different offices of the City while others are already returned to the GSO.
The above data revealed that a big percentage of unserviceable properties includes motor vehicles, construction and heavy equipment and IT equipment and software. There are items with notation in the remarks portion of the inventory report like“subject for repair” and others “scrap”.
Inquiry with the GSO revealed that they are still in the process of requiring all accountable officials or employees who were issued government property which have become unserviceable to prepare and submit the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) to his office so that it could immediately prepare a consolidated IIRUP.
Moreover, the GSO disclosed that based on the inventory conducted, he observed that most of the properties can no longer be repaired or reconditioned, or those which can be repaired require high replacement cost.
Timely and systematic disposal of unserviceable properties is necessary because it generates greater benefits to the City in terms of higher appraisal value for the properties, to avoid further deterioration of its value, lesser storage cost and better management of limited storage space.
Management commented that some of unserviceable properties were already disposed but were not taken out of the inventory report submitted.  A reconciliation of records will be made immediately.
Recommendation:
We recommended that the GSO:
Require all accountable officials and employees who were issued properties which have become unserviceable to submit the IIRUP so that it could immediately prepare the consolidated IIRUP of all unserviceable properties of the City;
Immediately file a request for disposal of unserviceable properties supported with necessary documents so that the required technical evaluation and appraisal could be made at once; and
Undertake promptly the disposal procedures pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. 1445.

Recommendation:

                 We reiterate  our  recommendation  that  the  City  General
           Services Officer (CGSO) or the Property Custodian conduct yearly
           physical count of the City properties and submit  a  RPCPPE  not
           later than 31 January of each year pursuant to  Section  124  of
           MNGAS,for LGUs, Volume I.

                 Moreover, the City Accountant and the  CGSO  implement  the
           reconciliation of their records inasmuch as this deficiency  has
           long been reported and no sincere  efforts  were  undertaken  to
           resolve the issue.

Recommendation:


                 We recommended that the  City  Accountant  discontinue  the
           practice of recording as  expense  the  supplies  and  materials
           based on  approved  POs  only  and  correspondingly  recognizing
           liability based thereon.



                 It is also recommended  that  the  City  Accountant  adhere
           strictly to the existing policy under Section 4.s  of  the  NGAS
           Manual for LGUs that liability shall be recognized at  the  time
           the  goods  and  services   are   accepted   or   rendered   and
           supplier/creditor bills are rendered.



                 Management promised  that  they  will  issue  a  memorandum
           informing all departments on the cut-off on  the  submission  of
           purchase requests for all supplies and materials.

Recommendation:


                 We recommended that Management instruct concerned officials
           to submit the copies of POs within the  reglementary  period  of
           five  (5)  working  days  upon  approval  together  with   their
           supporting document for review pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-
           001 dated February 12, 2009.

Recommendation:


                 We recommended that  the  City  faithfully  adhere  to  the
           provisions of RA 9184 in the procurement needs of the  City  and
           may resort to reimbursement  of  expenses  only  in  exceptional
           cases such as for petty operating expenses charged to the  Petty
           Cash Fund, payments out of the cash advance or reimbursement  of
           travel expenses.

Recommendation:

                 We recommended that the City Accountant ensure that PCF  is
           fully liquidated at year end and all expenses  are  recorded  in
           the year of occurrence pursuant to Section 48 of NGAS Manual for
           LGUs.

Recommendation:

                 We recommended that the City Accountant:

           a) Require the accountable officers to strictly comply with the
              guidelines provided in Items 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002
              and Section 89 of P.D. 1445.

           b) Send final demand notices to City Officials and employees
              whose cash advances as of December 31, 2011 remained
              unliquidated.

Recommendation:

                 We recommended that:

              1.  All cash advances chargeable against Confidential Fund and
                 Intelligence Fund shall be liquidated within 30 days  after
                 every quarter  in  compliance  with  Item  6.2.2  of  Joint
                 Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.






              2. The City Accountant or his duly  authorized  representative
                 coordinate with the COA Central Office and  follow  up  the
                 liquidation    of    cash    advances    charged    against
                 Intelligence/Confidential Fund.
